title: How We Learn
author: Benedict Carey
core_idea: Learning is not about perfect conditions and rigid discipline — it thrives on variation, breaks, and even forgetting. Carey reveals the science of memory and learning to show that many of our intuitions about studying are wrong.
key_concepts: Spacing effect: distributed practice beats cramming.
Testing yourself is more effective than rereading.
Interleaving different skills or topics improves retention.
Sleep is essential for memory consolidation.
Forgetting is not failure — it's part of the learning process.
notable_quote: Some forgetting... is not only permissible but desirable. It enables deeper learning when the material is restudied.
practical_lessons: Study in different locations to strengthen memory associations.
Take breaks — they help your brain consolidate information.
Mix up what you practice instead of drilling one skill repeatedly.
Test yourself frequently instead of just reviewing notes.
